title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Hannah Price, who runs a travelling food truck.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today I'm joined by Hannah Price, who runs a travelling food truck at markets and festivals. Hannah, you trained as a chef in a hotel, so why did you decide to work from a truck instead? || Guest: I loved cooking in a hotel kitchen, but I hardly met the people eating my food. With the truck, [20]I can hand a meal to a customer and hear immediately what they think of it[/20]. That contact was the main reason. || Interviewer: Was it easy to choose the kind of food you wanted to sell? || Guest: Not at all. I first thought about cakes, because I enjoy baking, and my brother suggested burgers. In the end, [21]I chose soups and hot sandwiches because they are simple to prepare in a small space[/21], and they suit cold outdoor markets. || Interviewer: What was the biggest problem when you started? || Guest: I worried about finding good places to park, but that was solved quite quickly. The surprise was the weather. [22]Rain can mean very few customers come, even if I've already bought all the ingredients[/22]. That's still hard to plan for. || Interviewer: Do you change your menu often? || Guest: Yes, but not every week. People like seeing something familiar. [23]I keep the most popular sandwich all year, then add one or two seasonal soups when vegetables are cheaper[/23]. It keeps things interesting without confusing regular customers. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy about festivals? || Guest: Festivals are tiring because the days are long, but they are fun. [24]I especially like the music and the friendly atmosphere while I'm working[/24]. It makes washing pans at midnight feel less boring. || Interviewer: What advice would you give someone who wants to start a food truck? || Guest: Don't spend all your money on a beautiful vehicle before testing your idea. [25]Try selling at a few small local events first, so you learn what people actually buy[/25]. You can improve the truck later. || Interviewviewer: Is there anything people misunderstand about your work? || Guest: They think I just cook for a few hours, but there is a lot before and after. I order food, drive to the site, clean everything, check gas bottles and answer emails from organisers. I also have to be cheerful even when I'm cold or tired. Still, I like being my own boss. || Interviewer: And where would you like the business to go next? || Guest: I don't want a restaurant, because that would mean staying in one place. I'd rather buy a second truck one day and train someone to run it. Then I could visit more events and maybe take proper holidays sometimes. || Interviewer: Do you ever change where you work because of customer habits? || Guest: Yes, definitely. Some markets look busy but people only buy coffee, while smaller school events can be better because families want proper food. I keep notes after every event, including the weather and what sold well. It sounds boring, but those notes help me decide where to return. || Interviewer: Has the job changed the way you cook at home? || Guest: It has made me simpler. At work I need food that is quick, warm and reliable, so at home I enjoy making slow meals that do not have to be served to a queue of hungry people.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today I'm joined by Hannah Price, who runs a travelling food truck at markets and festivals. Hannah, you trained as a chef in a hotel, so why did you decide to work from a truck instead?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I loved cooking in a hotel kitchen, but I hardly met the people eating my food. With the truck, I can hand a meal to a customer and hear immediately what they think of it. That contact was the main reason.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Was it easy to choose the kind of food you wanted to sell?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not at all. I first thought about cakes, because I enjoy baking, and my brother suggested burgers. In the end, I chose soups and hot sandwiches because they are simple to prepare in a small space, and they suit cold outdoor markets.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was the biggest problem when you started?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I worried about finding good places to park, but that was solved quite quickly. The surprise was the weather. Rain can mean very few customers come, even if I've already bought all the ingredients. That's still hard to plan for.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you change your menu often?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, but not every week. People like seeing something familiar. I keep the most popular sandwich all year, then add one or two seasonal soups when vegetables are cheaper. It keeps things interesting without confusing regular customers.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy about festivals?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Festivals are tiring because the days are long, but they are fun. I especially like the music and the friendly atmosphere while I'm working. It makes washing pans at midnight feel less boring.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give someone who wants to start a food truck?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Don't spend all your money on a beautiful vehicle before testing your idea. Try selling at a few small local events first, so you learn what people actually buy. You can improve the truck later.

dialogue.speaker: interviewviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything people misunderstand about your work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They think I just cook for a few hours, but there is a lot before and after. I order food, drive to the site, clean everything, check gas bottles and answer emails from organisers. I also have to be cheerful even when I'm cold or tired. Still, I like being my own boss.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: And where would you like the business to go next?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I don't want a restaurant, because that would mean staying in one place. I'd rather buy a second truck one day and train someone to run it. Then I could visit more events and maybe take proper holidays sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you ever change where you work because of customer habits?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, definitely. Some markets look busy but people only buy coffee, while smaller school events can be better because families want proper food. I keep notes after every event, including the weather and what sold well. It sounds boring, but those notes help me decide where to return.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has the job changed the way you cook at home?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It has made me simpler. At work I need food that is quick, warm and reliable, so at home I enjoy making slow meals that do not have to be served to a queue of hungry people.

questions.question: Why did Hannah decide to work from a food truck?

options.A: She wanted more contact with customers.
options.B: She could not find work in a hotel.

questions.explanation: Hannah says she wanted to hand food to customers and hear what they thought immediately.

questions.question: Why did Hannah choose soups and hot sandwiches?

options.A: They were suggested by her brother.
options.B: They were practical to make in the truck.
options.C: They were popular in the hotel where she trained.

questions.explanation: She says she chose them because they are simple to prepare in a small space.

questions.question: What problem did Hannah not expect when she started?

options.A: She could not find good places to park.
options.C: Bad weather reduced the number of customers.

questions.explanation: Hannah says rain can mean very few customers come, even after she has bought ingredients.

questions.question: How does Hannah organise her menu?

options.A: She keeps some dishes the same and changes others.
options.B: She offers a completely new menu each week.
options.C: She asks customers to choose the menu online.

questions.explanation: She says she keeps the most popular sandwich all year and adds seasonal soups.

questions.question: What does Hannah like about working at festivals?

options.A: The chance to finish work early.
options.B: The music and atmosphere around her.
options.C: The variety of food she can eat there.

questions.explanation: She says she especially likes the music and friendly atmosphere while she is working.

questions.question: What advice does Hannah give to people starting a food truck?

options.A: Buy the best vehicle they can afford.
options.B: Choose a menu before visiting events.
options.C: Test their idea at small events first.

questions.explanation: She advises people to sell at a few small local events first to learn what people buy.
